result.markdown:
--- title: "Data Report" format: html --- This report documents the data used in the project. It covers the raw datasets, the processing steps that produce the working data, and the exploratory analysis that informs the visualisations. The goal is to make the data side of the project traceable and reproducible for anyone joining later. ## Raw data ### Overview Raw Datasets : Overview of raw datasets used in the project. {#tbl-raw-overview} | Name | Source | Storage location | |---|---|---| | Instacart Online Grocery Dataset 2017 | Kaggle, published by Instacart | `data/` folder in the repository (CSV files, not committed) | ### Details: Instacart Online Grocery Dataset (2017) #### Description of content The dataset contains anonymised order data from Instacart, an online grocery delivery service in the United States. It covers more than 3.4 million orders placed by around 206,000 customers, and includes 49,688 unique products organised into 134 aisles and 21 departments. The full purchase history adds up to roughly 32 million product level entries. For each order the dataset records the day of week, the hour of day, the time since the previous order, the products bought, the position of each product in the basket, and whether the product had been ordered by the same customer before. #### Data source and provider The dataset was published on Kaggle by Instacart as part of a public competition and is widely used for research and educational purposes. The data is fully anonymised: customers and products only appear as numeric IDs, and no names, addresses, payment details or other personal information are included. #### Data procurement The raw CSV files are downloaded from Kaggle and placed in the `data/` folder of the repository. New team members can either download the files directly from Kaggle or run `data_acquisition/data_acquisition.ipynb`, which documents the expected file names and locations. #### Legal aspects and licensing The data is published under Kaggle's terms of service and Instacart's data usage rules. It may be used for research and educational work, but it must not be redistributed or used commercially. Anyone working with the repository is expected to follow Instacart's terms at https://www.instacart.com/legal/terms and Kaggle's dataset rules. #### Data governance The dataset is classified as public, fully anonymised and non personal. It contains no business confidential information from Instacart and no personally identifiable information from customers. No additional access controls or anonymisation steps are required on top of what Instacart already applied. #### Variable categorisation The variables used in the analysis fall into the following groups: * Dimensional variables for grouping and filtering: user ID, product ID, aisle, department, day of week, hour of day. * Measure variables for counting and aggregation: order counts per user or product, number of items per order, co-occurrence counts between products. * Target variable for loyalty analysis: the reorder rate per product, derived from the binary `reordered` column. * Explanatory variables: product attributes (department, aisle) and temporal attributes (day of week, hour of day). #### Entity Relationship Diagram ```{mermaid} erDiagram ORDERS ||--o{ ORDER_PRODUCTS_PRIOR : contains PRODUCTS ||--o{ ORDER_PRODUCTS_PRIOR : referenced_by AISLES ||--o{ PRODUCTS : categorises DEPARTMENTS ||--o{ PRODUCTS : categorises ``` The diagram shows the relational structure of the raw data. `orders.csv` is the order header table. `order_products__prior.csv` is the line item table linking orders and products. `products.csv` is the product master table, with `aisles.csv` and `departments.csv` providing the readable category labels. #### Data Catalogue : Data catalogue for the raw Instacart files. {#tbl-data-catalogue} | Column | Source file | Datatype | Range or validation | Short description | |---|---|---|---|---| | `order_id` | `orders.csv` | integer | unique per row | Identifier for one customer order. | | `user_id` | `orders.csv` | integer | not null | Anonymised customer identifier. | | `order_number` | `orders.csv` | integer | >= 1 | Sequence number of the order for that user. | | `order_dow` | `orders.csv` | integer | 0 to 6 | Day of week of the order. | | `order_hour_of_day` | `orders.csv` | integer | 0 to 23 | Hour of the order on a 24 hour scale. | | `days_since_prior_order` | `orders.csv` | float | 0 to 30, null for first order | Days between this order and the previous one for the same user. | | `order_id` | `order_products__prior.csv` | integer | foreign key to `orders.csv` | Order to which the line item belongs. | | `product_id` | `order_products__prior.csv` | integer | foreign key to `products.csv` | Product purchased in that order. | | `add_to_cart_order` | `order_products__prior.csv` | integer | >= 1 | Position of the product in the basket. | | `reordered` | `order_products__prior.csv` | integer | 0 or 1 | 1 if the user has ordered this product before, otherwise 0. | | `product_id` | `products.csv` | integer | unique per row | Product identifier. | | `product_name` | `products.csv` | string | not null | Human readable product name. | | `aisle_id` | `products.csv` | integer | foreign key to `aisles.csv` | Aisle the product belongs to. | | `department_id` | `products.csv` | integer | foreign key to `departments.csv` | Department the product belongs to. | | `aisle_id` | `aisles.csv` | integer | unique per row | Aisle identifier. | | `aisle` | `aisles.csv` | string | not null | Aisle label, for example "fresh vegetables". | | `department_id` | `departments.csv` | integer | unique per row | Department identifier. | | `department` | `departments.csv` | string | not null | Department label, for example "produce". | #### Data characteristics and quality The exploratory analysis is implemented in `eda/eda.ipynb`. The most relevant findings are summarised below. **Completeness.** The only missing values in the raw data appear in `days_since_prior_order`. The number of missing entries (206,209) matches the number of unique users in the dataset, which confirms that the missing values are not a quality defect but an expected artefact of the first order per user. All other columns are complete. **Univariate summaries.** The three numerical variables most relevant to the dashboard are profiled with standard descriptive statistics (mean, median, quartiles, range, standard deviation): `add_to_cart_order` as a proxy for basket size, `days_since_prior_order` as the key measure of shopping frequency, and `order_hour_of_day` as the basis for the temporal analysis. The summary is produced directly from the raw files: ::: {#tbl-univariate .cell tbl-cap='Univariate summary statistics for key numerical variables.' execution_count=1} ``` {.python .cell-code code-fold="true"} from pathlib import Path import pandas as pd data_dir = Path().resolve().parent / "data" orders = pd.read_csv(data_dir / "orders.csv") order_products = pd.read_csv(data_dir / "order_products__prior.csv") summary = pd.DataFrame( { "add_to_cart_order": order_products["add_to_cart_order"], "days_since_prior_order": orders["days_since_prior_order"], "order_hour_of_day": orders["order_hour_of_day"], } ).describe() summary ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=29} ```{=html}
add_to_cart_order days_since_prior_order order_hour_of_day
count 3.243449e+07 3.214874e+06 3.421083e+06
mean 8.351076e+00 1.111484e+01 1.345202e+01
std 7.126671e+00 9.206737e+00 4.226088e+00
min 1.000000e+00 0.000000e+00 0.000000e+00
25% 3.000000e+00 4.000000e+00 1.000000e+01
50% 6.000000e+00 7.000000e+00 1.300000e+01
75% 1.100000e+01 1.500000e+01 1.600000e+01
max 1.450000e+02 3.000000e+01 2.300000e+01
``` ::: ::: The variables behave as expected. `add_to_cart_order` is right skewed because most baskets contain a small number of items but a few baskets are very large. `days_since_prior_order` is bounded at 30 by the way Instacart records the value. `order_hour_of_day` shows the daily shopping rhythm and is the basis for the heatmap in @fig-shopping-heatmap. **Categorical variables.** The categorical variables (`department`, `aisle`) have a small and fixed number of classes (21 departments, 134 aisles). The distribution is heavily skewed: a few departments such as Produce and Dairy & Eggs account for most of the purchase volume, while Bulk, Other and Alcohol contribute very little. **Reorder behaviour.** The overall reorder rate sits at around 58 per cent. Products with reorder rates above 0.8 are everyday staples such as milk, water and bananas. This pattern is the main signal used in the loyalty view of the dashboard. **Co-purchase patterns.** Classical correlation measures (Pearson, Spearman, Kendall) are not very informative for this dataset, since the analysis works with categorical product IDs rather than numerical features. The equivalent role is played by co-occurrence counts and lift values between product pairs. The strongest co-occurrence at department level is Dairy & Eggs with Produce, which appears in roughly 1.8 million orders. **Temporal patterns.** Most orders are placed on Sundays and Mondays between 09:00 and 15:00, with very low activity before 06:00 and after 22:00. The heatmap in @fig-shopping-heatmap visualises this rhythm. **Limitations.** The dataset has no absolute calendar dates, only day of week and hour of day, so no calendar based trend analysis is possible. The data also reflects U.S. shopping behaviour from 2017 and is not necessarily representative of current or European customers. ## Processed data ### Overview Processed Datasets : Overview of processed datasets used in the project. {#tbl-processed-overview} | Name | Source | Storage location | |---|---|---| | Merged product master table (`products_full`) | `products.csv` merged with `aisles.csv` and `departments.csv` | Created in memory in `deployment/data_loaders.py` (`load_csv`) and `eda/*.ipynb` | | Per-product statistics (`product_stats`) | `order_products__prior.csv` grouped by `product_id`, enriched with `products_full` | Created in memory in `deployment/data_loaders.py` (`product_stats`) | | Aisle aggregates (`aisle_aggregates`) | `order_products__prior.csv` grouped by `(department, aisle)`, enriched with `products_full` | Created in memory in `deployment/data_loaders.py` (`aisle_aggregates`) | | Aisle co-purchase pairs (`top_aisle_pairs`) | Aisle-level co-occurrence counts and lift values derived from `order_products__prior.csv` | Created in memory in `deployment/data_loaders.py` (`top_aisle_pairs`, `dept_pair_matrix`) | ### Details: Merged Product Master Table #### Description The merged product table combines the product master data with readable aisle and department labels. It is the basis for every view in the dashboard that shows products by category, since the raw `products.csv` only contains numeric foreign keys. #### Processing steps 1. Load `products.csv`, `aisles.csv` and `departments.csv`. 2. Merge `products.csv` with `aisles.csv` on `aisle_id`. 3. Merge the result with `departments.csv` on `department_id`. 4. Keep the result as `products_full` in memory. The merge is cheap and deterministic, so the table is rebuilt at runtime rather than persisted to disk. #### How to access ```python products_full = ( products .merge(aisles, on="aisle_id") .merge(departments, on="department_id") ) ``` #### Data Catalogue | Column | Datatype | Range or validation | Short description | |---|---|---|---| | `product_id` | integer | unique per row | Product identifier. | | `product_name` | string | not null | Human readable product name. | | `aisle_id` | integer | foreign key | Aisle the product belongs to. | | `aisle` | string | not null | Aisle label. | | `department_id` | integer | foreign key | Department the product belongs to. | | `department` | string | not null | Department label. | ### Details: Per-Product Statistics #### Description This table aggregates purchase behaviour at the individual product level. It is the single source of truth for "how often is product X bought, and how loyal are its customers?" and is used by the Top Sellers, Reorder Rate, and Hidden Gems views in the dashboard. #### Processing steps 1. Group `order_products__prior.csv` by `product_id`. 2. Compute `count` (total number of times the product was purchased) and `reorder_rate` (mean of the binary `reordered` column). 3. Compute `share_pct` as each product's count divided by the total number of line items. 4. Merge with `products_full` to add `product_name`, `department`, and `aisle`. The table is rebuilt at runtime from the cached `load_csv()` result. #### How to access ```python stats = ( order_products.groupby("product_id") .agg(count=("product_id", "size"), reorder_rate=("reordered", "mean")) .reset_index() ) stats["share_pct"] = stats["count"] / stats["count"].sum() * 100 product_stats = stats.merge( products_full[["product_id", "product_name", "department", "aisle"]], on="product_id", ) ``` #### Data Catalogue | Column | Datatype | Range or validation | Short description | |---|---|---|---| | `product_id` | integer | foreign key | Product identifier. | | `count` | integer | >= 1 | Total number of purchases across all orders. | | `reorder_rate` | float | 0 to 1 | Share of purchases that were reorders. | | `share_pct` | float | 0 to 100 | This product's share of all line items. | | `product_name` | string | not null | Human readable product name. | | `department` | string | not null | Department label. | | `aisle` | string | not null | Aisle label. | ### Details: Aisle Aggregates #### Description This table pre-aggregates purchase count and reorder statistics at the `(department, aisle)` level. It reduces the 32-million-row line item table to 134 rows and is used by the Department Sales views. #### Processing steps 1. Join `order_products__prior.csv` with `products_full` on `product_id` to attach `department` and `aisle`. 2. Group by `(department, aisle)`. 3. Compute `count` (line items), `reorder_sum`, `reorder_rate`, and `share_pct`. #### How to access ```python op = order_products.merge( products_full[["product_id", "department", "aisle"]], on="product_id", ) aisle_aggregates = ( op.groupby(["department", "aisle"]) .agg(count=("product_id", "size"), reorder_sum=("reordered", "sum")) .reset_index() ) aisle_aggregates["reorder_rate"] = aisle_aggregates["reorder_sum"] / aisle_aggregates["count"] aisle_aggregates["share_pct"] = aisle_aggregates["count"] / aisle_aggregates["count"].sum() * 100 ``` #### Data Catalogue | Column | Datatype | Range or validation | Short description | |---|---|---|---| | `department` | string | not null | Department label. | | `aisle` | string | not null | Aisle label. | | `count` | integer | >= 1 | Total purchase line items for this aisle. | | `reorder_sum` | integer | >= 0 | Number of reordered line items. | | `reorder_rate` | float | 0 to 1 | Share of purchases that were reorders. | | `share_pct` | float | 0 to 100 | Aisle's share of all line items. | ### Details: Aisle Co-Purchase Pairs #### Description This dataset captures how often pairs of aisles appear in the same order, and expresses the strength of each association as a lift value. It is the analytical basis for the Products Bought Together view and the department-level co-occurrence heatmap in the dashboard. #### Processing steps 1. For each order, collect the set of distinct aisles it contains. 2. For each unordered pair of aisles `(a, b)`, count the number of orders in which both appear (`co_orders`). 3. Compute lift as `co_orders × n_orders / (count_a × count_b)`, where `count_a` and `count_b` are the individual order frequencies of each aisle. 4. Filter out pairs below a minimum count (default 1,000) and a minimum lift (default 1.3). 5. For the dashboard heatmap, aggregate the pair counts into a symmetric department × department matrix (`dept_pair_matrix`). The heavy counting step (`compute_aisle_pairs`) runs once and is cached by Streamlit. All downstream views receive the cached result. #### Data Catalogue | Column | Datatype | Range or validation | Short description | |---|---|---|---| | `aisle_a` | string | not null | First aisle in the pair. | | `department_a` | string | not null | Department of `aisle_a`. | | `aisle_b` | string | not null | Second aisle in the pair. | | `department_b` | string | not null | Department of `aisle_b`. | | `co_orders` | integer | >= min_count | Number of orders containing both aisles. | | `share_of_orders` | float | 0 to 100 | Co-orders as a percentage of all orders. | | `lift` | float | >= min_lift | Lift value: observed co-occurrence divided by expected under independence. | ## Exploratory Analysis The underlying exploratory work is in `eda/eda.ipynb`. Rather than listing findings as bullet points, this section asks the four questions that drove the dashboard's design and shows the data behind each answer. The goal is to make the path from raw numbers to business decision visible, what was found, why it matters, and how it is reflected in the final product. ### What sells? The most direct question for any assortment planner is which products actually move. @fig-top-products shows the 20 most-purchased products across 32 million purchase events in `order_products__prior.csv`. ::: {#cell-fig-top-products .cell execution_count=2} ``` {.python .cell-code code-fold="true"} from pathlib import Path import matplotlib.pyplot as plt import matplotlib.ticker as mticker import seaborn as sns import pandas as pd data_dir = Path().resolve().parent / "data" products = pd.read_csv(data_dir / "products.csv") order_products = pd.read_csv(data_dir / "order_products__prior.csv", usecols=["product_id"]) top_products = ( order_products.groupby("product_id")["product_id"] .count() .reset_index(name="count") .merge(products[["product_id", "product_name"]], on="product_id") .sort_values("count", ascending=False) .head(20) ) BAR_COLOR = "#FB6A4A" sns.set_theme(style="whitegrid") plt.rcParams["axes.titleweight"] = "bold" fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6)) sns.barplot(data=top_products, x="count", y="product_name", color=BAR_COLOR, ax=ax) ax.set_xlabel("Number of purchases") ax.set_ylabel("") ax.xaxis.set_major_formatter(mticker.FuncFormatter( lambda v, _: f"{v/1e6:.1f}M" if v >= 1e6 else f"{v:,.0f}")) ax.bar_label(ax.containers[0], fmt="{:,.0f}", padding=3, fontsize=8) sns.despine(left=True, bottom=True) plt.tight_layout() plt.show() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![Top 20 products by purchase volume.](data_report_files/figure-html/fig-top-products-output-1.png){#fig-top-products width=945 height=560} ::: ::: Bananas are the single most-purchased product by a wide margin. Thirteen of the top 20 carry an organic label, pointing to a platform-wide preference for fresh and organic produce that goes beyond individual product popularity. This skew shapes the department picture as well: @fig-dept-sales shows that Produce alone accounts for nearly nine million purchases, almost twice as many as the next largest department, Dairy & Eggs. ::: {#cell-fig-dept-sales .cell execution_count=3} ``` {.python .cell-code code-fold="true"} from pathlib import Path import matplotlib.pyplot as plt import seaborn as sns import pandas as pd data_dir = Path().resolve().parent / "data" products = pd.read_csv(data_dir / "products.csv") aisles = pd.read_csv(data_dir / "aisles.csv") departments = pd.read_csv(data_dir / "departments.csv") order_products = pd.read_csv(data_dir / "order_products__prior.csv", usecols=["product_id"]) products_full = (products .merge(aisles, on="aisle_id") .merge(departments, on="department_id")) dept_counts = ( order_products .merge(products_full[["product_id", "department"]], on="product_id") .groupby("department")["product_id"] .count() .reset_index(name="count") .sort_values("count", ascending=False) ) dept_counts["count_M"] = dept_counts["count"] / 1_000_000 BAR_COLOR = "#FB6A4A" sns.set_theme(style="whitegrid") plt.rcParams["axes.titleweight"] = "bold" fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6)) sns.barplot(data=dept_counts, x="count_M", y="department", color=BAR_COLOR, ax=ax) ax.set_xlabel("Number of purchases (millions)") ax.set_ylabel("") ax.bar_label(ax.containers[0], fmt="%.1fM", padding=3, fontsize=8) sns.despine(left=True, bottom=True) plt.tight_layout() plt.show() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![Total purchases by department.](data_report_files/figure-html/fig-dept-sales-output-1.png){#fig-dept-sales width=945 height=560} ::: ::: Produce and Dairy & Eggs are the two anchor departments. Snacks and Beverages follow at a considerable distance; departments like Bulk, Other, and Alcohol rarely appear in orders on this platform. **For assortment planners:** These two charts form the non-negotiable baseline. Produce and Dairy & Eggs are not just popular, they are the reason customers open the app. Any assortment change that touches either carries outsized risk. The **Top Sellers** and **Department Sales** views in the dashboard present exactly this data in an interactive format that can be filtered and explored before planning meetings. ### Who keeps coming back? Purchase frequency tells us what sells; reorder rate tells us who stays. @fig-reorder-rate ranks the products with the highest reorder rates among items purchased at least 500 times, the minimum to make the rate statistically meaningful. ::: {#cell-fig-reorder-rate .cell execution_count=4} ``` {.python .cell-code code-fold="true"} from pathlib import Path import matplotlib.pyplot as plt import seaborn as sns import pandas as pd data_dir = Path().resolve().parent / "data" products = pd.read_csv(data_dir / "products.csv") order_products = pd.read_csv(data_dir / "order_products__prior.csv", usecols=["product_id", "reordered"]) product_counts = order_products.groupby("product_id")["product_id"].count() popular = product_counts[product_counts >= 500].index reorder = ( order_products[order_products["product_id"].isin(popular)] .groupby("product_id")["reordered"] .mean() .reset_index() .rename(columns={"reordered": "reorder_rate"}) .merge(products[["product_id", "product_name"]], on="product_id") .sort_values("reorder_rate", ascending=False) .head(20) ) BAR_COLOR = "#FB6A4A" sns.set_theme(style="whitegrid") plt.rcParams["axes.titleweight"] = "bold" fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6)) sns.barplot(data=reorder, x="reorder_rate", y="product_name", color=BAR_COLOR, ax=ax) ax.set_xlabel("Reorder rate") ax.set_ylabel("") ax.set_xlim(0, 1) ax.axvline(0.8, color="#888888", linestyle="--", linewidth=1) ax.bar_label(ax.containers[0], fmt="%.2f", padding=3, fontsize=8) sns.despine(left=True, bottom=True) plt.tight_layout() plt.show() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![Top 20 products by reorder rate (minimum 500 purchases). The dashed line marks 80%.](data_report_files/figure-html/fig-reorder-rate-output-1.png){#fig-reorder-rate width=945 height=560} ::: ::: Every product in the top 20 exceeds 80% reorder rate: customers who buy them once buy them again, predictably. The list is dominated by everyday staples, milk, water, bananas, where purchasing is habitual rather than exploratory. This is the platform's loyalty core. Strong reorder rates are not, however, exclusive to bestsellers. @fig-hidden-gems plots reorder rate against purchase volume for every product with at least 100 purchases, separating the five highest-volume departments from the rest of the assortment. ::: {#cell-fig-hidden-gems .cell execution_count=5} ``` {.python .cell-code code-fold="true"} from pathlib import Path import matplotlib.pyplot as plt import seaborn as sns import pandas as pd data_dir = Path().resolve().parent / "data" products = pd.read_csv(data_dir / "products.csv") aisles = pd.read_csv(data_dir / "aisles.csv") departments = pd.read_csv(data_dir / "departments.csv") order_products = pd.read_csv(data_dir / "order_products__prior.csv", usecols=["product_id", "reordered"]) products_full = (products .merge(aisles, on="aisle_id") .merge(departments, on="department_id")) overall_reorder = order_products["reordered"].mean() product_stats = ( order_products.groupby("product_id") .agg(count=("product_id", "size"), reorder_rate=("reordered", "mean")) .reset_index() .merge(products_full[["product_id", "department"]], on="product_id") ) top5_depts = ( product_stats.groupby("department")["count"].sum() .sort_values(ascending=False).head(5).index.tolist() ) product_stats["segment"] = product_stats["department"].isin(top5_depts).map( {True: "Top-5 departments", False: "Other departments"} ) plot_df = product_stats[product_stats["count"] >= 100] BAR_COLOR = "#FB6A4A" sns.set_theme(style="whitegrid") fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 5)) sns.scatterplot( data=plot_df, x="count", y="reorder_rate", hue="segment", palette={"Top-5 departments": "#D9D9D9", "Other departments": BAR_COLOR}, s=12, alpha=0.4, edgecolor="none", ax=ax ) ax.set_xscale("log") ax.axhline(overall_reorder, color="#555555", linestyle="--", linewidth=1) ax.text(plot_df["count"].max() * 0.5, overall_reorder + 0.03, f"overall reorder rate ({overall_reorder:.0%})", fontsize=8, color="#555555") ax.set_xlabel("Number of purchases (log scale)") ax.set_ylabel("Reorder rate") ax.set_ylim(0, 1) ax.legend(title="") sns.despine() plt.tight_layout() plt.show() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![Reorder rate versus purchase volume per product (log scale). Products outside the five highest-volume departments are highlighted. The dashed line marks the overall platform reorder rate (~58%).](data_report_files/figure-html/fig-hidden-gems-output-1.png){#fig-hidden-gems width=933 height=462} ::: ::: Hundreds of low-volume products outside the dominant departments exceed the platform's overall reorder rate of roughly 58%. These are the hidden gems of the assortment: niche items, specialty wines, artisan breads, premium deli meats, with small but highly loyal customer bases that standard sales reports make invisible. **For buyers:** A supplier negotiation requires more than a top-seller list. The scatter above makes a quantitative case for products that score on loyalty rather than volume, the starting point for targeted promotions, subscription nudges, or retention-focused campaigns. The dashboard's **Reorder Rate** and **Hidden Gems** views make this analysis interactive and filterable. ### What belongs next to what? Raw co-purchase counts tell us which categories are large, not which ones genuinely belong together. When Produce appears in every top department pair, that reflects its size, not a meaningful affinity. Replacing raw frequency with **lift** removes this size bias: a lift of 2.0 means a pair appears together twice as often as chance would predict given each category's individual popularity. @fig-aisle-lift shows the 15 strongest aisle-level associations by lift, restricted to pairs with at least 2,000 shared orders to exclude fragile combinations. ::: {#cell-fig-aisle-lift .cell execution_count=6} ``` {.python .cell-code code-fold="true"} from pathlib import Path from itertools import combinations from collections import Counter import matplotlib.pyplot as plt import seaborn as sns import pandas as pd data_dir = Path().resolve().parent / "data" products = pd.read_csv(data_dir / "products.csv", usecols=["product_id", "aisle_id"]) aisles = pd.read_csv(data_dir / "aisles.csv") op = pd.read_csv(data_dir / "order_products__prior.csv", usecols=["order_id", "product_id"], dtype={"order_id": "int32", "product_id": "int32"}) pid2aid = dict(zip(products["product_id"], products["aisle_id"])) aid2name = dict(zip(aisles["aisle_id"], aisles["aisle"])) op["aisle_id"] = op["product_id"].map(pid2aid).astype("int16") op = op[["order_id", "aisle_id"]].drop_duplicates() n_orders = int(op["order_id"].nunique()) count_a = op.groupby("aisle_id")["order_id"].size().to_dict() grouped = op.groupby("order_id")["aisle_id"].agg(list) pair_counts: Counter = Counter() for ais in grouped: for a, b in combinations(sorted(set(ais)), 2): pair_counts[(a, b)] += 1 MIN_CO_ORDERS = 2000 drop_names = {"missing", "other"} rows = [] for (a, b), c in pair_counts.items(): if c < MIN_CO_ORDERS: continue name_a = aid2name.get(a, str(a)) name_b = aid2name.get(b, str(b)) if name_a in drop_names or name_b in drop_names: continue lift = c * n_orders / (count_a[a] * count_a[b]) rows.append({"aisle_1": name_a, "aisle_2": name_b, "co_orders": c, "lift": lift}) top_lift = ( pd.DataFrame(rows) .sort_values("lift", ascending=False) .head(15) ) top_lift["pair_label"] = top_lift["aisle_1"] + " + " + top_lift["aisle_2"] BAR_COLOR = "#FB6A4A" sns.set_theme(style="whitegrid") plt.rcParams["axes.titleweight"] = "bold" fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(11, 6)) sns.barplot(data=top_lift.sort_values("lift"), x="lift", y="pair_label", color=BAR_COLOR, ax=ax) ax.axvline(1.0, color="#555555", linestyle="--", linewidth=1) ax.set_xlabel("Lift (1.0 = independent; higher = bought together more than chance predicts)") ax.set_ylabel("") ax.bar_label(ax.containers[0], fmt="%.1f×", padding=3, fontsize=8) sns.despine(left=True, bottom=True) plt.tight_layout() plt.show() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![Top 15 aisle pairs by lift (minimum 2,000 shared orders). The dashed line marks lift = 1.0 (statistical independence).](data_report_files/figure-html/fig-aisle-lift-output-1.png){#fig-aisle-lift width=1041 height=560} ::: ::: The lift ranking reveals three clear shopping-basket themes that raw counts miss entirely. Wine aisles dominate the top: red and white wines are bought together roughly 40 times more often than chance would predict. Below the alcohol cluster, a personal care theme emerges (oral hygiene, deodorants, hair care), followed by a baking cluster (baking supplies paired with dough and mixes) and a household cleaning cluster (laundry with cleaning products). None of these relationships would surface from a frequency ranking, where every top pair would simply include Produce. This aisle-level lift analysis is the analytical foundation of the dashboard's **Aisles Bought Together** page. The shopping-basket themes visible in the bar chart correspond directly to the colour-coded communities in the interactive network graph, the chart and the network are two views of the same underlying structure. ### When do customers shop? The dataset records no absolute calendar dates, only day of week and hour of day. @fig-shopping-heatmap encodes order volume across both dimensions simultaneously, a pattern that any single average or marginal distribution would obscure. ::: {#cell-fig-shopping-heatmap .cell execution_count=7} ``` {.python .cell-code code-fold="true"} from pathlib import Path import pandas as pd import matplotlib.pyplot as plt import seaborn as sns data_dir = Path().resolve().parent / "data" orders = pd.read_csv(data_dir / "orders.csv") heatmap_data = ( orders.groupby(["order_dow", "order_hour_of_day"])["order_id"] .count() .unstack() ) heatmap_data.index = [ "Sunday", "Monday", "Tuesday", "Wednesday", "Thursday", "Friday", "Saturday", ] fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 4)) sns.heatmap( heatmap_data, cmap="YlOrRd", ax=ax, linewidths=0.4, linecolor="white", cbar_kws={"label": "Number of orders"}, ) ax.set_title("Order frequency by day and hour", fontsize=14, fontweight="bold", pad=12) ax.set_xlabel("Hour of day") ax.set_ylabel("") plt.tight_layout() plt.show() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![Order frequency by day of week and hour of day.](data_report_files/figure-html/fig-shopping-heatmap-output-1.png){#fig-shopping-heatmap width=1070 height=368} ::: ::: Orders cluster sharply on Sundays and Mondays between 09:00 and 15:00, the weekly grocery planning window. Activity drops near zero before 06:00 and after 22:00 on every day of the week. The peak is not a gradual rise but a concentrated band, consistent with deliberate weekly planning rather than impulsive shopping. Promotional campaigns timed to this window reach customers when they are actively placing orders; late-evening promotions would find almost no audience. ### From data to decisions The four findings above describe the same customer from different angles: a habitual, health-conscious buyer who anchors every visit in Produce and Dairy, shops on a predictable Sunday-to-Monday morning schedule, builds strong routines around a handful of staples, and occasionally discovers a niche product they then purchase repeatedly. The dashboard is designed to make exactly these patterns accessible without requiring either Sandra or Marco to run queries or produce charts themselves. Each page in the dashboard corresponds directly to one of the four questions answered here: **Top Sellers** and **Department Sales** for volume, **Reorder Rate** and **Hidden Gems** for loyalty, **Aisles Bought Together** for co-purchase structure, and **Shopping Time Heatmap** for temporal patterns. The exploratory analysis is not separate from the product, it is the product, made interactive. ## Summary The project uses the Instacart 2017 dataset as its only source of data. The raw files are clean and well documented, with the only missing values being the expected ones for each user's first order. The processing layer adds readable category labels to the products. Together with the raw files, this covers every analysis shown in the dashboard. The data is sufficient for the visualisation goals of the project, with the understanding that all results are bound to U.S. shopping behaviour in 2017 and to within-week temporal patterns.
